## #1 — #1 Elon Musk: Tesla, SpaceX, & the Quest for a Fantastic Future

Founders #1 Elon. Only listen to this episode to learn about Senra, not Elon. There is so much value in understanding the origins of the man who occupies your ear canals for hours every week. The episode is raw and unpolished, however Senra is still Senra.

I am going to listen to every episode of Founders from the very beginning, posting my thoughts from one episode daily.

## #2 — #2 Walt Disney

Founders #2 Walt Disney.

What would Disney think of Sora? In some ways, he played a significant part in the story. What he created and the people he inspired with animation and in cinema ended up becoming a meaningful chunk of Sora's data.

Before his fame, Disney worked seven days a week for three years developing Snow White, with everyone, including his wife, doubting him and telling him that nobody would ever watch a 2-hour cartoon. It broke every box-office record and changed cinema forever.

No doubt a young Disney would adopt these tools today.

## #3 — #3 The Wizard of Menlo Park: How Thomas Edison Invented The Modern the Modern World

Founders #3 Thomas Edison. “The names change, the technology changes, but human nature usually stays the same…“ -Senra
At the end of Edison’s life, he constantly drank milk, convinced it would cure all his ailments. Would Edison find oat milk as cool as some people do today? Maybe.

First mention of AI in the show is about five minutes in.

## #4 — #4 The Remarkable Life and Turbulent Times of Joseph P. Kennedy

Founders #4 Joseph P. Kennedy. A fascinating man who pushed every rule to the limit to achieve the unique (in terms of Founders) goal of generational power through politics. When you listen to this episode, one thing that Senra only briefly flashed on was that Joseph heavily relied on his networking prowess to move between banking, film production, stock trading, and eventually politics for himself first and later his kids. Yes, he was insanely focused, but what sticks out is that he was willing to do anything and everything to get what he wanted (market manipulation, working with the mob, etc.)

Rating: 3/10 (my opinion based on a mix of personal value and entertainment, see for yourself)

Prior episodes:
Musk: 7/10 (really good if you love Senra as much as I do)
Disney: 8/10 (amazing storytelling by Senra and criminally underrated founder)
Edison: 4/10 (lacks depth into key moments outside of the very end, kinda boring)

## #5 — #5 Steve Jobs

Founders #5 Steve Jobs. This episode serves as a fantastic refresher for anyone looking to learn from Jobs. Many of Senra’s later maxims are in proto-form in this one. Jobs refused to accept automatic truths from day one (First Principles). In design, Jobs was obsessed with simplicity. This also applied to Job’s communication style, which Senra breaks down in great detail in #281 and #349. First discussions of A players vs B players.

Towards the end of the episode, Senra highlights the innovator's dilemma and how Jobs feared falling for it. There is almost a paradoxical element towards the idea, as even if you know it occurs, you can still lose. For the greatest CEOs/Founders, it boils down to who you surround yourself with (i.e., avoiding yes men) and learning how to execute a succession plan properly.

7/10. An 8/10 if I were further along in the journey and had more confidence. The Disney episode was better imo.

## #6 — #6 Sam Walton

Founders #6 Sam Walton. An interesting wrinkle in Walmart’s success is the timely arrival of commercial private aviation: 1) scouting the expansion in rural markets, a key component of Walmart's thesis; and 2) enabling Walton to monitor every location on the ground, a unique attribute of Walton that he would use throughout his leadership. 

5/10

## #7 — #7 Grinding It Out: The Making of McDonald's

Founders #7 Ray Kroc.

1) Great ideas often stem from constraints. Before buying out the McDonald brothers, Kroc had limited flexibility to change the business, as every decision needed written approval from both brothers. Many key innovations came from this limitation, the most significant being purchasing or leasing sites for McDonald’s restaurants and then renting them to franchisees. Kroc gained steady cash flow and full control over them, since breaking McDonald’s standards could mean losing the lease. Today, $MCD ranks as the fifth-largest corporate landlord in the world by asset value.

2) Any time is the right time to become an entrepreneur. Kroc was 52 when he joined McDonald’s.

3) When Ray was training for Vietnam, he was with a man in his camp who would spend his weekends on base drawing pictures instead of going out with the others. That man was Walt Disney. (11:38)

4) How you do one thing reflects how you do all things . (40:30)

5) “Business is not like painting a picture, you cannot put a final brushstroke and then hang it on the wall…” Kroc remained deeply involved in McDonald’s until he passed away. (55:00)

6/10

## #8 — #8 The Intel Trinity: How Robert Noyce, Gordon Moore, and Andy Grove Built the World's Most Important Company

Founders #8 Robert Noyce 

1) Noyce’s anti-bureaucratic flat culture, combined with a wave of young talent, made $INTL the symbol of disruption and capable of meeting its own seemingly impossible standards for innovation (Moore’s Law).

2) “The names change, the technology changes, but human nature usually stays the same” (27:30), Senra reiterates the maxim first introduced in episode #3 (Edison).

3) An overlooked bubble: 1960s integrated circuits and calculators. A flood of new entrants, collapsing prices, and failing companies. I can see how the promise of a handheld device that could save hours of work and impact daily life made for the perfect entry point for the masses to adopt integrated circuit devices.

## #9 — #9 I Invented the Modern Age: The Rise of Henry Ford

Founders #9 Henry Ford

Ford had one idea for 20 years and refused to let it go. Everyone should be able to afford a car.

You don’t actually need fifty ideas. You need one clear vision, driven by a level of violence in execution (s/o Gen. Patton), through mass-production supremacy, with cost-cutting serving as a driver of both profits and marketing.

## #10 — #10 Shoe Dog: A Memoir by the Creator of Nike

Lessons from Founders #10 Phil Knight, founder of $NKE

1) “History is one long processional of crazy ideas.” (7:36)

2) What Phil calls the secret of happiness, “Play… The secret of happiness, I'd always suspected, the essence of beauty or truth or all we ever need to know of either, lay somewhere in that moment when the ball is in midair, when both boxers sense the approach of the bell… There's a kind of exuberant clarity in that pulsing half-second before winning and losing are decided. I wanted that, whatever that was, to be my life, my daily life.”

3) Bowerman (Nike co-founder) was obsessed with losing weight; it was a simple equation: “‘One ounce sliced off a pair of shoes,' he said, 'is the equivalent to 55 pounds over one mile.’” Bowerman eventually designed the original waffle shoe, not before becoming the coach of the US Olympic track team.

Simplicity in goals (or lightness for Bowerman) clarifies decision-making.

4) You should do what you love says Knight. Senra adds, “I'm shocked at how many people don't even try.” Underrated point.

5) “If you’re following your calling, the fatigue will be easier to bear. The disappointments will be fuel. The highs will be like nothing you've ever felt.” PK (63:00)

8/10

2000s: Bezos uses that fortune to fund Blue Origin.

#86: Carl Bosch & Fritz Haber

The Haber-Bosch process is considered by some to be the most important discovery ever made, as it essentially converts air (nitrogen) into synthetic fertilizer that now sustains the lives of billions. Without this technology, it is estimated that over 2 billion people would face starvation.

## #14 — #14 The Accidental Billionaires: The Founding of Facebook: A Tale of Sex, Money, Genius, and Betrayal

Founders #14 Mark Zuckerberg (Accidental Billionaires)

## #19 — #19 Becoming Steve Jobs

Day 19 listening to every @FoundersPodcast episode

#19 Becoming Steve Jobs

A key lesson Jobs learned at Pixar was that the work should not be driven by the deadline and that you must keep at the difficult parts until they are perfected.

“At Pixar, on almost every film they make, something turns out to be not quite right. And they have an amazing willingness to turn around and do it again, till they do get it right. They have always had a willingness to not be governed by the release date. It's not about how fast you do something, it's about doing your level best." - Steve Jobs (52:19)

Will Rockstar deliver?

Given the track record, I believe they will. It takes a ton of courage to delay this multiple times as a public company.

## #20 — #20 Danny Meyer (The Transforming Power of Hospitality in Business)

Founders #20: Danny Meyer (Shake Shack, USHG)

Honestly, not my favorite episode. I think when this was recorded, Shake Shake was at its peak of popularity. That's not to say it should discredit what Senra is highlighting or what Meyers did. I found the episode to lack a punch. Nice quote about handling adversity: “The road to success is paved with mistakes well handled” (41:37). Afterwards Senra highlights Meyer’s belief that pursuing perfection is costly. I understand the point, but it's more interesting that it contradicts exactly what I highlighted in #19 from Steve Jobs’ time at Pixar.

## #21 — #21 Masters of Doom: How Two Guys Created an Empire and Transformed Pop Culture

Founders #21: John Carmack and John Romero (Doom)

This episode is really two founder origin stories running in parallel until they collide. Carmack is a cracked technical founder who had this open-source “hackers” philosophy, which led to their revolutionary video game distribution strategies that paved the way for the freemium model. Romero was the charismatic spokesman who had strong business instincts, who also overcame a brutal upbringing, which is highlighted in detail.

Senra uses them to demonstrate how far you can go with this classic pairing, but also how easily things can unravel. Overall, this episode is more about storytelling than quick punchlines (which isn't a bad thing), and I think it's better than #20.

## #22 — #22 How To Turn Down A Billion Dollars: The Snapchat Story

Founders #22: Evan Spiegel, $SNAP

What is privacy? Spiegel believes privacy is primarily “understanding of context, not what is said, but where it is said and to whom. Privacy allows us to enjoy and learn from the intimacy that is created when we share different things with different people and different contexts.” Senra ()

Social media is the result of capitalism getting into our personal relationships. ()

## #23 — #23 The New New Thing: A Silicon Valley Story

Founders #23: Jim Clark (Netscape)

“You reach these points in life where you say, fuck, I’ve reached some sort of dead end here. What was the point of getting here? … All those years you thought you were achieving something, and you achieved nothing.” (29:22) Clark was 38 at the time. It’s never too late to become a billionaire. Clark soon thereafter founded Netscape with Marc Andreessen, who Senra recommends as an “interesting” follow on Twitter (33:26). Given he was beefing with the Pope on here just a couple days ago, I cannot agree more with the rec. 

Clark argued there were two types of people: 1) the pig, who is committed; and 2) the chicken, who is interested. “If you are going to do anything worth doing, you need a lot of pigs.” I think the point here is commitment versus curiosity and the importance of being able to decipher who is what when hiring. (43:30)

Adding to my first point, the guy basically hit rock bottom at 38 after his second divorce, then one night decided to lock in after facing the worst two years of his life (he had a terrible childhood btw). Clark was always trying to prove everyone wrong. What a beauty.

## #28 — #28 The Wright Brothers

Founders #28: Wright Brothers

The Wright Brothers were voracious readers; there was always room for books in the family’s modest budget (7:00). Often they reread books for retention (18:38). The latter point is why I write these posts. 

The dual benefit of having a social contract with myself to commit to this daily process, combined with reflective note-taking, creates a repository of the points I found most valuable. 

People (myself included) often go through the motions, listening to podcasts, so having that added expectation of writing something for a small audience every single day.

Senra’s own take on reading: “The entire reason I do the podcast because I do believe if people read more, their lives would be better… you can spend 5-20 hours having a one-sided conversation with somebody that's thought deeply about the subject so much that they spend years of their life writing the book.” (7:20)

## #33 — #33 Levi Strauss: The Man Who Gave Blue Jeans to the World

Founders #33: Levi Strauss (Levi Jeans)

When Levi Strauss ventured West, he faced a journey of 3 to 6 months with no guarantee of success. These dangerous, one-way journeys are no longer a reality for most of us.

The risk is contained nowadays, yet human nature still creates the same apprehension. The only actual difference is that today, you can decide 12 days into a "journey" to turn around.

When you set out to try something new, doubt is inevitable. I believe a key to success in modern society is to stick things out. Would you ask the captain to turn around? Hell no.

(30:32)

Tokenomics as "Compelling" behavior (exploitation of retail investors using alt coins) instead of "Educing" behavior (people using it because of shared purpose or utility). ## #46 — #46 I Love Capitalism: An American Story

Founders #46 Ken Langone - Home Depot $HD

Langone was a classic American come up, born in 1935 to parents without a high school diploma between them, Ken climbed the ladder, first college at Bucknell, then Wall Street. While a summary, one thing that stood out about Ken was his great intuition and ability to find opportunities, demonstrated in a more “realistic” way (i.e., we are not our own boss).

For example, early on while working on the Street as a salesman, Langone was desperate for cash, he managed to connect with a small insurance company and pitched helping with an IPO. Langone, still in his first year, went back to the top banker at his firm (remember Langone is in sales) and essentially pressured them into the deal, netting a hefty fee. Langone went on to become a partner, eventually leaving. Decades later, he founded Home Depot and became a billionaire.

The story I just told was a small chunk of the man’s story. Senra wraps up the episode with a fitting final takeaway from Langone, stating, “One of the most important lessons in my life is this. Leave more on the table for the other guy than he thinks he should get.” (1:06:00)

This may sound like a minor feat relative to other podcast subjects, but to be this bold so early in one's career is a reminder of what we can do. Don't wait until your next big move or jump; see what you can do now in your current employment situation. There's no point in waiting to do the "big bold moves". At worst, it's fantastic practice.

@ethanmonkhouse 100%. There’s no point in waiting.

## #50 — #50 Marc Andreessen's Blog Archive

Founders #50 Marc Andreessen

The market is the most critical factor in a startup's success or failure, beating out both the caliber of the team and the quality of the product. A great market with lots of customers will pull the product out of the startup, but a terrible market will kill even the best team with the best product. (14:30)

Don’t plan your career. The world changes too quickly for you to predict what will happen. It is better to treat your career like an investment portfolio where you balance risks and are always ready to seize opportunities that present themselves spontaneously. (36:00 - 50:00)

When Thomas Edison invented the phonograph, the New York Times predicted it would be used for "bottled sermons" where wealthy people would collect recorded religious speeches, almost no one initially foresaw it becoming a device for mass entertainment. (32:30)

You should view big companies like Moby Dick because their internal decision-making is complex and completely unpredictable to outsiders. Never start a company that requires a partnership with a big corporation to succeed, as they can easily capsize you without even meaning to. (20:30)

Mel (Banana Republic founder) goes on a silent Buddhist retreat specifically to cut off communication with Bill (first time founder). ## #59 — #59 Howard Hughes: The Definitive Biography of the First American Billionaire

Founders #59 Howard Hughes (aerospace engineer, film producer, business magnate, and world record pilot)

The foundation of the Hughes empire was the work of Howard Hughes Sr., a man mechanical by nature who rebelled against a desk job to find fortune in the “frontier of opportunity”.

Howard Hughes Jr is on his own at 18 years old. Following the sudden death of his father, the teenage Hughes seized total control of the family business.

Despite owning a massively profitable tool company, Hughes’s lack of financial oversight and personal extravagance led to a liquidity crisis.

During World War II, Hughes partnered with industrialist Henry Kaiser (also a fascinating guy) to build the world's largest flying cargo ship to avoid enemy submarines.

The crash of the XF-11 spy plane in Beverly Hills marked the definitive end of Hughes's vibrant life and the start of his opioid addiction. It was his fourth crash.

He reminds me of Wilt Chamberlain, a god-gifted talent, but so self-centered that he never reaches his full potential. Wilt also liked to drive fast.

## #60 — #60 Yvon Chouinard: What We've Learned from Patagonia's First 40 Years

Some additional data points from the @FoundersPodcast episode:

Doing good is actually good business (11:25).

The best businesses have a point of view (13:20).

Something is wrong with your company if you are constantly hiring externally when promotions are available (16:35).

To produce a bad product is to do bad business (26:00).

Founders #60 Yvon Chouinard (Patagonia)

In 2016, JPMorgan offered Databricks $10M to bring the platform on-prem. At the time, the company was sub $1M ARR. Founder/CEO Ali Ghodsi decline. They still ignore the on-prem market despite massive demand and revenue opportunities. If they had broken this cloud-only vision, I am sure the company would not be growing at 55% y/y at a $4.8B runrate. As a public company where every quarter matters, shareholder pressure to capture on-prem would be immense as soon as there is a bad quarter.

So, what does this have to do with Patagonia?

Patagonia would have failed if it went public in its first 40 years.

In the 1970s, Patagonia (then Chouinard Equipment) phased out its best-selling product, pitons (metal spikes for climbing), because they realized the gear was permanently damaging rock faces. Pitons accounted for 70% of their business. A public company cannot kill its main revenue source for "moral reasons." Eventually their aluminum chocks (which didn’t damage the rocks) became a bigger business and greatly strengthened brand loyalty.

In the 1980s, Malinda Chouinard drove the implementation of high-quality on-site child care. It was expensive and showed no clear ROI on a spreadsheet, but it led to incredibly low turnover and a more "human" workplace. This would have been unacceptable to shareholders in the 1980s, especially since Patagonia was struggling financially at the time.

In 1996, upon realizing conventional cotton was one of the most toxic crops on earth, Patagonia switched 100% of its sportswear line to organic cotton in just 18 months. This tripled their material costs and forced them to reduce their product line from 91 styles to 66. Public markets would have demanded a slower, "phased" rollout to protect quarterly earnings. Chouinard insisted on the "all-in" approach because he refused to be complicit in environmental damage.

After raising the $4B Series L, Ali Ghodsi stated that “In 2021, a lot of my peers that took their companies public, then in 2022 investors demanded EBITDA, so they all did layoffs and they cut down on all the futuristic stuff, innovation, and new products… We are now growing twice the growth rate of those companies.” (source: @tbpn)

## #61 — #61 Malcom McLean: The Box: How the Shipping Container Made the World Smaller and the World Economy Bigger

#61 Malcolm McLean (The Father of Modern Shipping)

McLean pioneered modern container technology and built a business around it that reduced handling costs and time, making global trade much more efficient. Early on, McLean faced fierce resistance despite the clear advantages, as ports hesitated to adopt his system, with dockworkers fearing mass job losses due to “automation.” 

JFK said at that time on the topic: “I regard it as a major domestic challenge really of the 1960s, to maintain full employment at a time when automation is replacing them.” Prophetic. In 2024, there was a massive port strike involving over 47K dockworkers across 36 ports, the largest since 1977. History rhymes.

People don’t want the technology, they want the solution it provides (4:55)

It sometimes takes an outsider to change an industry. Malcom came from the trucking sector. (8:20)

There was a lot of resistance, but the genie was out of the bottle (10:58).

Rational people don't risk what they have and need for what they don't have and don't need. (32:20)

Sometimes it’s better not to overanalyze, or you’ll talk yourself out of it (49:05).

Starting with a roughly $400M net worth in 1982, McLean wiped out his wealth and accumulated about $1.3B in personal debt by 1986 by investing his exit funds into an oil container ship business. It’s only too late if you copy (1:12:42).

## #62 — #62 The Autobiography of Benjamin Franklin

Founders #62: Benjamin Franklin

Below are Franklin’s 13 Virtues, my favorites are Industry, Frugality, and Humility.

Temperance – Eat not to dullness, drink not to elevation.
Silence – Speak not but what may benefit others or yourself, avoid trifling conversation.
Order – Let all your things have their places, let each part of your business have its time.
Resolution – Resolve to perform what you ought, perform without fail what you resolve.
Frugality – Make no expense but to do good to others or yourself, waste nothing.
Industry – Lose no time; be always employed in something useful, cut off all unnecessary actions.
Sincerity – Use no hurtful deceit, think innocently and justly, and, if you speak, speak accordingly.
Justice – Wrong none by doing injuries or omitting the benefits that are your duty.
Moderation – Avoid extremes, forbear resenting injuries so much as you think they deserve.
Cleanliness – Tolerate no uncleanliness in body, clothes, or habitation.
Tranquility – Be not disturbed at trifles or at accidents common or unavoidable.
Chastity – Rarely use venery but for health or offspring, never to dullness, weakness, or injury of your own or another’s peace or reputation.
Humility – Imitate Jesus and Socrates.

I have more datapoints I have from this episode, but I am saving those for a broader compilation post.

## #65 — #65 Kirk Kerkorian: Penniless Dropout became the Greatest Deal Maker in Capitalist History

Founders #65: Kirk Kerkorian (MGM Grand)

Despite becoming a billionaire, Kirk never depended on armies of lawyers for agreements. He considered a handshake a binding agreement. In one case, he turned down a higher offer for a company because he had already shaken on a lower one the day before. (7:39)

Kirk built his initial capital by flying dangerous missions for the Royal Air Force over the North Atlantic during WWII, taking a risky route with a high fatality rate because it paid roughly $1,000 per month (24:40).

Kirk consistently reinvested profits from one venture into the next. After pivoting to buying surplus military planes, refurbishing them, and selling them for parts or as complete units for huge returns, he then built a flight school. He sold that and started a charter airline, which he later flipped into real estate in Las Vegas.

Kirk believed there was no point in placing small bets and always to “bet the limit”. He built the largest hotel in Las Vegas three times during his career, reaching a peak net worth of around $20B in 08. He donated almost all his wealth after his death at age 98.

## #69 — #69 Charles Goodyear: An Inventor's Obsession and the Struggle for a Rubber Monopoly

Founders #69: Charles Goodyear (creator of vulcanized rubber)

If you are interested in market bubbles, here’s my primer on a sleeper pick beyond the scope of the podcast.

In 1833, America fell into a "Rubber Fever," convinced that this new miracle substance was the universal material for the future. While waterproof shoes were legitimately hyped, the craze quickly spiraled into absurdity: businessmen pitched rubber roofs that would never leak and rubber carriage bodies that would never rot. It wasn't just seen as a new product; optimists viewed it as a fundamental replacement for wood, leather, metal, and more.

Capital flooded into the sector to fund "The Monster," a 30-ton steam-powered machine designed to ramp up production immediately. The market was so intoxicated by the theoretical possibilities that they ignored the lack of basic durability testing. They scaled up factories before the chemistry had actually stabilized. (This was the “Peak of Inflated Expectations” stage of the Gartner Hype Cycle.)

The reality check came during the heatwave of 1834, exposing a catastrophic flaw: untreated rubber melts. The "impervious" rubber roofs turned into black sludge dripping onto residents, and the government-contracted mailbags fused into solid bricks that had to be split apart with axes. The miracle material turned out to be thermally unstable. The collapse was total. Stock prices hit zero, factories closed, and rubber became a synonym for fraud. The entire sector evaporated as quickly as it had appeared.

It took a bankrupt obsessive named Charles Goodyear, experimenting in a jail cell, to finally discover vulcanization years later. The industry eventually recovered and changed the world, but the real breakthrough didn't happen during the speculative boom.

Key note: the Goodyear tire company was founded years later by a different man, Frank Seiberling, who named it after Charles Goodyear. This episode is really a cautionary tale, as Charles failed to capture the value he created.

## #77 — #77 Steve Jobs (The NeXT Years)

He hired a full-time interior designer as one of his first ten employees and spent more time on his high-end office design than on key product decisions. He abandoned the pressure of frugality from the start.

At a major education conference, NeXT had no prototype to display, so Jobs instead handed out an 18-page brochure explaining the company's new logo.

Jobs ignored the failure of the Apple Lisa, repeating the same mistakes. Universities explicitly told Jobs they needed a computer under $3,000, but he dismissed their advice and built a machine costing between $7,000 and $10,000.

Jobs insisted on manufacturing the hardware in-house in San Francisco because he believed customers would travel to the factory to watch their computer being built, like a BMW.

When engineers tried to report realistic delays, Jobs would berate them, causing his team to lie about timelines to avoid his anger.

While the company was struggling to sell computers, Jobs spent 20 minutes arguing with landscapers about sprinkler placement while executives waited for him.

Jobs claimed NeXT had its first profitable quarter (heavily adjusted), fooling the public and his own employees into thinking the business was healthy just before laying off over 60% of the staff.

This was the only correct decision in the episode, as dropping the hardware division was critical, but it was not Jobs’ idea.

Founders #77: Steve Jobs (NeXT Era)

If you want to know exactly what not to do when running a startup, study NeXT. He basically front ended every possible mistake before his nearly perfect second run at $AAPL.

See below for the highlight reel:

@fordsmith 100%. It was like when Michael Jordan played baseball before his second three-peat.

It was a mistake, but nobody is complaining about the success that followed.

## #87 — #87 Thomas Watson (IBM)

Founders #87: Thomas Watson - $IBM

"I don't know if this joke is going to land, but I think Watson was the Kanye West of his day." (43:00)

Prophetic insight by Senra that has aged like fine wine since the episode was uploaded, thanks to one insane twist.

Why? Here are a few things Thomas Watson did:

He wrote about himself in the third person.

He claimed to have a "pronounced gift for public speaking" and stated he was "continually being called upon to exercise it" when he was mainly the one calling upon himself to speak to his employees.

When filling out a survey about himself, he described himself as having “Tremendous vision, indomitable will, unswerving determination”.

He described himself as having a "tall figure with dark, direct eyes that appeared to see through people and situations at first glance."

Twist:
Not mentioned in the episode, Watson was more or less business partners with Hitler until it was considered not cool. He even received the Merit Cross of the German Eagle in 1937 from the German government, which he famously declined in 1940.

The episode isn't very insightful compared to previous ones. So, I figured I would just share this absurdity.

## #92 — #92 Ed Thorp and Claude Shannon

Lessons from past experiences often lead to unexpected benefits. (10:45) The Kelly Criterion is a proportional betting system that requires scaling your bet size relative to your current cash balance, ensuring you never risk total ruin. This is achieved by always betting a calculated fraction of your bankroll, increasing your stake as you win and decreasing it as you lose. This makes it impossible to lose all your money.

This goes hand in hand with the Rule of Inevitability. Even unlikely events must eventually happen. If your strategy allows for ANY risk of total ruin, regardless of how small, and you play long enough, you'll lose it all. In reality, an example would be fat tail scenarios in financial markets. (28:00)

If you don't have an edge, you don't bet. (41:00, 1:16:00)

Cool fact: Warner Media Group was started by two mobsters in Vegas who owned a collection of parking lots. The business grew into the $70B+ pending acquisition by $NFLX.

"There's no rule in life that's saying that other humans are going to play by the rules. And if you don't understand that, you can put yourself in a very dangerous situation." - David Senra (43:00)

Pay attention to what people do versus what they say. (46:00)

Founders: #92 Ed Thorp and Claude Shannon

Ed Thorp developed card counting and was the godfather of quant hedge funds.

Claude Shannon was the creator of Information Theory and the computer term “bit”.

Blunt reminder of the cautionary tale some of the greatest founders share: “A man dominated by a passion like mine with the risks, with the time it requires, cannot be a good husband and above all, cannot be a good father.” - Enzo Ferrari (45:30)

Lasting legacies are often built on the foundations of painful and uncertain pivots. Often where one dream must die for a greater one to emerge. (34:00)

Money only solves money problems. (23:30)
