## #100 — #100 Warren Buffett (The Snowball)

Founders: #100 Warren Buffett

“When you get to my age, you'll really measure your success in life by how many of the people you want to have love you actually do love you... I don't care how big your bank account is. The only way to get love is to be lovable.” - Warren Buffett

(1:24:30)

Generously sharing what you know is one of the highest leverage activities. (9:30)

Buffett learned from his father to live by an “inner scorecard”, judging himself by his own standards rather than an “outer scorecard” that relies on external validation or public perception. (12:00)

When starting his investment partnership, Buffett refused to show his partners his specific stock picks because he wanted to avoid people co-tailing his trades, unlike his mentor Ben Graham who was transparent (and hurt his returns). (50:30)

“You walk down the street, and you see a cigar butt. And it's kind of soggy and disgusting and repels you, but it's free, and there may be one puff left in it.” Buffett on undervalued but low quality businesses (55:00)

Intensity is the price of excellence. (59:15)

Work for who you admire most. (1:17:30)

Be thoughtful with key decisions. Buffett viewed life as limited to a punchcard with only punches (decisions) in your life. (1:20:30)

## #101 — #101 Warren Buffett (The Tao of Warren Buffett)

Founders: #101 Warren Buffett - $BRK

“You want to learn from experience, but you want to learn from other people's experiences when you can.” (28:30)

Frugality must be an ingrained habit rather than a temporary, reactionary measure taken only when a business faces trouble. (30:00)

How you do one thing is how you do all things. (32:30)

Don’t swing at the ball when it’s still in the pitcher’s hand. (38:30)

## #102 — #102 Akio Morita (Sony)

Founders: #102 Akio Morita - $SONY

“If you go through life convinced that your way is always best, all the new ideas in the world will pass you by.”

From the rubble of a bombed-out warehouse to a global titan, Sony is the definitive story of Japan’s return to the world stage: 

When Sony started, the global perception of Japanese-made goods was terrible. Sony’s series of global hit products, such as the Walkman, helped change “Made in Japan” from a stigma to the highest mark of quality. (36:00)

Founder vs Founder debate on market research:

Morita did not believe in market research. “Our plan is to lead the public with new products rather than ask them what kind of products they want... The public does not know what is possible, but we do.” (39:15)

This idea contrasts with David Ogilvy, known as the “godfather of advertising,” who advised relying on your market research, much like a surgeon does:

“Why should our clients be expected to bet millions of dollars on your intuition? This willful refusal to learn the rudiments of the craft is all too common.” - Founders #82 (15:20, 38:05)

Ogilvy’s belief in market research has a key nuance: “Use research as a drunk uses a lamppost, not for illumination but for support.”

I believe Morita’s take on market research is more pointed toward the market research dogma that likely fails to grasp Ogilvy’s consideration.

Does relying on data kill the ability to build things people didn't know they wanted?

Additional short datapoints from Akio Morita and $SONY

Physical neglect is visible in the mirror (a feedback loop), mental neglect is invisible but just as damaging. (48:45)

When Akio was in his 70’s he had three ingredients to keep his youthful spirit:
1) Hang out with enthusiastic young people
2) Vigorous exercise
3) Maintain confidence in yourself (52:00)

To identify poor managers, Akio created a weekly internal newsletter with job postings so employees could confidentially transfer between departments. This makes it easier to spot underperformers and retain talent. (54:30)

Wisdom is not the exclusive possession of management. (55:30)

Actions express priorities. (1:02:00)

## #103 — #103 Hetty Green (The Richest Woman in America)

Founders #103 Hetty Green - Investor

“The markets may change, the methods may be revamped, but as long as human beings are propelled by greed and ego, they are doomed to repeat the mistakes of the past.”

In 1907, she single-handedly bailed out New York when major banks failed:

Her ideas on managing your money:

“Watch your pennies and the dollars will take care of themselves.” (2:45)

When offered a bargin, always take the evening to reflect before accepting the deal. (3:30)

Most people watch their money wash away in the flood. (18:00)

She was an all-american badass:

Hetty meets with rival Collis Huntington (a partner of Leland Stanford), but the meeting turns hostile when he threatens her son, Ned, causing her to drop her business persona and pull a gun on Huntington. (47:30)

Charlie Munger’s inversion thinking… but from 100 years before:

As Hetty ages and hands over control to her son, she provides him with a list of “don'ts” rather than “dos”. Here is the list:

Don't cheat in business dealings. “For sooner or later, conscious will trouble you and your worries up until into your grave.”
Don't fail to be fair in all things, both in business and otherwise.
Don't kick a man when he is down.
Don't envy your neighbors.
Don't overdress, whether you have the means or not, because this causes envy.
Don't fail to go to church.
Don't forget that riches dishonorably gained must be left behind someday.
Don't forget to be charitable.
Don't falsify.
(58:00)

## #104 — #104 Ingvar Kamprad (IKEA)

Founders #104 Ingvar Kamprad - IKEA

Think of time wasted in minutes not hours or days.

“Time is your most important resource... 10 minutes once gone are gone for good. Divide your life into 10-minute units and sacrifice as few of them as possible.” (1:17:15).

9 Rules of IKEA:

1. Product range is our identity.
Don’t create a product and hope people buy it, identify your target customer’s constraints (ex. price). Your product range is your primary communication tool, so if it is confusing, your brand identity will be affected too.

2. The IKEA spirit.
Build a living culture based on your natural roots or spirit. IKEA leadership is expected to work on the floor with employees.

3. Profit gives us resources.
Treat profit as the price of your independence. External capital means less control.

4. Reaching good results with small means.
Constraints are the mother of innovation. McDonald’s real estate empire was built through constraints from the original founders. As a result, Ray Kroc turned $MCD into the fifth-largest corporate landlord in the world by asset value. (Founders #7)

5. Simplicity is a virtue.
IKEA fights complexity by keeping the organization flat and the rules short. Complexity kills speed.

6. Doing it a different way.
Question every requirement aka first principles thinking.

7. Concentration.
Focus your resources on specific targets rather than spreading them too thin.

8. Taking responsibility.
“Only those who are asleep make no mistakes... .. Making mistakes is the privilege of the active.” - Kamprad (32:00)

9. A glorious future.
Avoid stagnation by believing that the majority of the work still remains to be done. Kamprad’s approach to tackling the Innovator's Dilemma.

Other datapoints:

Great way to learn what you want to do is by learning what you don’t want to do first. (10:30)

Kamprad deeply regrets missing his kids childhood for work. “Childhood does not allow itself to be reconquered.” (26:15)

“The decisive moment in the growth of a company is when the founder has to hand over the financial function to somebody else.” (50:45)

Full quote on time: “Bear in mind your time, time is your most important resource... 10 minutes is not just 1/6th of your hourly pay, 10 minutes is a piece of yourself. Divide your life into 10-minute units and sacrifice as few of them as possible in meaningless activity.” (1:17:15)

## #105 — #105 Les Schwab (Charlie Munger recommended this book)

Founders #105 Les Schwab

Aim to know more than anyone else about your specific craft. Schwab believed this in the 1950s. With the internet, there’s no excuse for you not to be the most knowledgeable person in your industry. (23:15)

A good salesman is a magician. Learn sales. (29:30)

Nobody works extra hard just because they like you. Align your business goals with the personal interests and ambitions of your employees. (48:00)

“Life is hard for people who think they can take a shortcut.” (55:15)

“If we fail to create, then we will die on the vine like so many other companies have done in the past.” (1:18:00)

## #106 — #106 Bill Walsh (The Score Takes Care of Itself)

Founders #106 Bill Walsh - NFL

“Champions behave like champions before they’re champions. They have a winning standard of performance before they are winners.”

Your org must have a clear structure and philosophy before focusing on results. “The score will take care of itself.”

If you love sports, this episode is a must-listen. If not, you should still know Bill Walsh's principles.

More on standards:

The principles of a high-performance organization are universal. (16:15)

Like water, many decent individuals will seek lower ground if left to their own inclinations. Humans require high standards to succeed. (36:00)

The Success Disease. After winning multiple Super Bowls, the media labeled Walsh a genius. He identified this praise as an “albatross around my neck” that could lead to arrogance and distract him from the hard work that got him there. 
You must maintain your own internal scorecard (Buffett) and refuse to let external praise or criticism define your reality. (50:15)

Extreme success often comes with an emotional toll. Knowing when to walk away often decides whether a Founder’s story becomes a cautionary tale of sacrifice. Walsh retired at the peak of his career, right after winning his third Super Bowl, because the internal pressure of maintaining his “Standard of Performance” had become too high to sustain. He chose to walk away to protect himself instead of letting the stress ruin him, turning down blank check offers from across the NFL. (1:04:00)

The score will take care of itself. “A pretty package can’t sell a poor product.” Walsh realized winning was the only way he could convince fans to buy tickets. (1:03:00)

Other notes on Bill Walsh:

You have an obligation to teach everything you know to those around you. (14:00)

“A game changer takes a while to change the way the game is played.” Initially Walsh faced a lot of criticism for his West Coast offense. Walsh pressed on. 40 years later, his influence can still be seen on the field. (44:00)

Don’t waste time thinking about your competitors. (48:30)

There is no true mastery, it’s a process. Never stop improving. (53:30)

“In evaluating people, I prize ego. It often translates into a fierce desire to do their best and an inner confidence that stands them in good stead when things really get rough.” (55:00)

@Chancellorpen Love to hear it. It’s a fantastic, fast-paced episode. Winning multiple Super Bowls as an NFL coach is one of the most evident signs of an exceptional leader. Managing that many personalities while requiring an insane level of attention to detail is outrageous.

@Chancellorpen 100%. That also applies to evaluating other people. Actions express priorities, not words.

@Chancellorpen You probably have me beat there. 0.05% is next level.

## #107 — #107 Sol Price (Costco)

Founders #107 Sol Price

“I've stolen as many ideas from Sol Price as from anybody else in the business.” Sam Walton, Walmart

He also influenced Jim Sinegal (Costco) and Jeff Bezos.

Price founded and took three companies public. He was also a great father. Here are his ideas.

You win if your kid reflects on your life like this. 

“As much as Sol's public accomplishments represent a tremendous legacy, his more enduring legacy may be the impact he had on the lives of the people who knew him through personal or business relationships. All of us can honestly say that he had a transformative impact… The greatest tribute I can give him is that he taught me so much, sometimes without even realizing a lesson was taking place.” - Robert Price (1:05:00)

On the importance of teaching.

“If you're not spending 90% of your time teaching, you're not doing your job.”

“You train an animal, you teach a person..”

He believed that manuals were a substitute for thinking. (38:00-39:45)

Origin of membership discounting in retail. 
The specific mechanism to legally offer discount prices was taken from Eugene Ferkauf, the founder of E.J. Korvette in New York from the 1940s. At the time, fair-trade laws allowed manufacturers to legally force retailers to sell products at a minimum price, preventing discounting. 

Ferkauf discovered that he could circumvent these price-fixing laws by requiring shoppers to sign up for a membership. Sol applied this exact strategy to FedMart, selling a $2 lifetime membership that legally classified his store as a private club. This legal distinction allowed him to ignore manufacturer pricing mandates and sell goods at whatever lower price he chose. (22:00)

This would have occurred otherwise, but it’s interesting to think that Eugene inspired Price, who then taught the strategy to Walton $WMT and Sinegal $COST, who then inspired Bezos $AMZN to launch Prime.

Other pieces.

While working long, grueling hours at a manufacturing plant during WWII, Sol discovered a work ethic that sharply contrasted with his father's laziness. (12:00)

Do it now, there is always enough time. Price had a sign on his desk that said “do it now.”(13:30)

Structuring deals to limit your maximum loss gives you the courage to attempt uncertain ventures. (26:00)

## #108 — #108 Jim Simons (Money Printer)

Founders #108: Jim Simons - Renaissance Technologies

Even world-class intellects aren’t immune to impostor syndrome. Simons nearly quit a decade into his second career before launching the greatest fund of all time, which delivered a 66% average annual return from 1988 to 2018.

Simons nearly gave up before he became the greatest fund manager ever.
At around 42 years old, Jim Simons had already left a prestigious, tenured career in mathematics to pursue trading full-time.

After a decade of trading, his new algorithmic system fails disastrously, causing his fund to hemorrhage money.

Facing angry clients and deep personal humiliation, he and his team lose faith in their thesis, leading Simons to explicitly question whether he should abandon the entire idea of automated trading.

The path to outlier success is through the valley of death where you temporary logic and emotion suggest quitting. (33:00-39:00)

How to get back on track.
He doubled down on his algorithmic trading, relying on his core competency in mathematics, and launched his flagship Medallion Fund in 1988, which has now earned over $100 billion in trading profits since then.

Two decades prior, Simons’ son died tragically. He learned then that adversity is rarely unique; it’s a universal human experience, so keep pushing through the pain. (11:30)

How to manage feeling overwhelmed.
Simons would lie motionless in a dark room for hours, letting his mind wander through complex problems. I have found that even 15-30 minutes spent like this is a powerful tool. Wear an eye mask and ear plugs. (8:00)

Markets always evolve.
Markets are biological, not mechanical.

Your edge is always eroding because competitors (invasive species) are constantly entering your territory, requiring you to perpetually evolve to survive. (55:00)

Emperors want empires.
You don't get into this business to make a little bit of money. You want to conquer the world. (56:00)

“Be guided by beauty. It can be the way a company runs, or the way an experiment comes out. There's a sense of beauty when something is working well.” (1:03:30)

@Chancellorpen 100%. One of the brightest minds featured on Founders, yet it took Simons 20 years for RenTech to achieve significant success.

## #109 — #109 Adi Dassler (Adidas)

Founders #109 Adi Dassler - Adidas

True innovation often appears as common sense in hindsight.

Adi noticed Olympic runners wore generic shoes.

Adidas dominated the podium. Even the Soviets chose the capitalist spikes.

Sounds obvious 100 years later, but it wasn’t back then:

Family conflicts formed blind spots that Nike exploited.
The Dassler family was so caught up in their internal feud between Adidas and Puma (Adi's brother's company) that they completely overlooked the threat of a new American competitor gaining market share. (50:00)

While $ADS targeted elite athletes, $NKE saw the huge potential in expanding the market to everyday people, effectively creating the jogging category.

The biggest growth often comes not from better serving existing customers (elite athletes), but from redefining who the customer is (making everyone an athlete). (1:00:00)

Nike forgot this concept when $LULU took over the everyday consumer market by establishing the rise of athleisure wear in the 2010s.

@Chancellorpen I agree, but admit I need to learn more about Puma’s story.

@zachtdavidson Right! The Soviets painted over the classic white stripes to try to hide them. It fooled no one.

## #110 — #110 Henry Singleton (Teledyne)

Founders #110 Henry Singleton - Teledyne

Identify your strengths and ignore your competitors.

Teledyne avoided markets where they had no advantage.

Instead focused on areas where they could dominate with technical superiority.

$TDY had ~20% annual returns from 1960 to 1990:

Resource allocation, not just capital.
Effective capital allocation depends on first assigning the right human resources. (14:45)

Singleton on spin-offs.
“There are companies that will sell one division and buy another because today, this division generally supports a low multiple and the one they're buying has a high multiple. And they think that may rub-off on the whole company. That absolutely turns me off. That whole concept is repulsive. We don't do things like that. We look at the economic long-term possibilities.” (1:18:00)

Other data points.
Opportunities are limitless, no matter the era. (12:15)

There’s no single way to do anything, never forget that. (42:00)

Moving into financial services allowed the company to act as its own bank, providing a capital base to fund future growth and acquisitions. (58:30)

@Chancellorpen Singleton was a genius in every way.

## #111 — #111 David Geffen

Founders #111: David Geffen

“Anybody who thinks money will make you happy, hasn't got money” - David Geffen

He lied, cheated, and screamed his way to a billion-dollar empire.

The only soul he had was in the records he sold.

He is a case study in how to win the wrong game.
 
He tried to engineer his own mythology.

Geffen desperately wanted a biography to canonize him as a business hero like Warren Buffett.

Yet by abusing the author, he ironically ensured the opposite happened.

The book instead became a permanent testament to his sociopathic ruthlessness.

Geffen's life was marked by immense financial success, matched only by his regret.

From his youth, primary filter for life decisions was monetary reward rather than interest or talent.

This set a precedent for his life, where he constantly prioritized money over fulfillment, leading to long-term dissatisfaction. (24:30)

He loved the film industry, but left it for music. I bet if he stuck it out he would have been equally successful doing what he loved.

He is the first subject in Founders to lie to everyone for an advantage.

He later told his biographer: “I don't have the burden of bullshit lies, and deceit and cheating none of that plagues me because I don't do any of it” (1:02:00)

Oh the irony.

“Socialpaths sleep well” - David Senra

When you write, don't overuse AI.

“Anything you do to optimize your work, cut corners or squeeze more efficiency out of it, will eventually make you dislike it.” - Nassim Taleb (6:00)

@Chancellorpen Absolutely. Geffien’s artist catalog was generational. The way he saved Calvin Klein with little effort was remarkable.

## #112 — #112 Frank Lloyd Wright

Founders #112: The Greatest American Architect

“If there is no spiritual quality in architecture, it would just be plain lumber.” - Frank Lloyd Wright

Cut corners, and you lose the spirit. 

Soul is the difference between a builder and an artist.

Here is why he stands alone.

Frank Lloyd Wright was a creative anomaly.

He peaked in his 80s.

In just his final decade, he produced 350 designs.

That accounted for a third of his entire life's work.

Works from his second peak include: Fallingwater, the Guggenheim Museum, and the Johnson Wax Building (pictured below).

“Ordinary people cannot live without rules to guide their conduct. I, Frank Lloyd Wright, am not ordinary.” (13:30)

This peak came after spending the previous two decades in despair.

He was broke and lacked respect from his own industry.

Wright reflected:

”My obituaries are all of such nature as to make me want to arise and fight... The comeback seems to be a favorite American institution. From now on, you are to find me actively engaged in that performance.” (1:10:00)

Wright's opinion of himself and architecture.
“If I said, I was the greatest architect in the world, I don't think it'd be arrogant.” (10:00)

“Architecture is the triumph of human imagination over materials, methods, and men, to put man into possession of his own earth.” (11:00)

## #113 — #113 A.G. Gaston (Black Titan and the Making of a Black American Millionaire)

Founders #113

A.G. Gaston grew up in a log cabin in Alabama with grandparents who had been slaves.

After serving in WWI, Gaston, with only a 10th-grade education, built an estate worth over $200M.

The question he always asked was, “A young person should keep his eyes open. They should study the people around them. How do they live? What makes them tick, what do they need?” (24:45)

## #114 — #114 The Professor, the Banker, and the Suicide King: Inside the Richest Poker Game of All Time

Founders #114: Andy Beal

Attention is our most valuable resource, yet we squander it.

Every second of your day and every ounce of your energy should be directed toward your desired position in life.

Treat every lie you tell yourself as a slap in the face.

“It's easier to stick to your principles 100% of the time than it is 98% of the time.” - Clayton Christensen (34:30)

Relish tough competition. (1:02:15)

Avoid self-sabotage. Ego grows fast. (1:08:00)

Embrace the visceral physical reaction to one’s own failures. (1:12:30)

Identify where they have a unique advantage and compete in those arenas.

Refuse to compete in arenas where you are weak. (18:30)

@Chancellorpen “If people say I'm doing something crazy, that's usually a good sign” - Andy Beal

There needs to be a full biography on him someday.

## #115 — #115 Ben Franklin: An American Life

Founders #115 Ben Franklin

Young Franklin cured his argumentative nature by adopting the Socratic method.

He found that humble inquiry guides others to your point far more effectively than aggressive debate.

The lesson: Stop the abrupt contradiction. Instead simply ask why.

Franklin's ideas of success and growth.
Success is measured by the positive utility provided to the public. (9:00)

Growth is the direct result of gaining knowledge, and conquering adversity. (16:30)

The more you learn, the more interesting you become.

-> The nuance lies in discovering what interests you; don’t push a rope trying to learn fine art. There is a balance between authenticity and external appeal. (23:15)

Mediocrity is always invisible until passion shows up and exposes it. (26:00)

Reputation is built on both reality and perception.

Franklin was strategic about when he performed visible acts of hard work as a signal of reliability.

He understood the tradeoff between when to “show up” and when his time was better spent on other tasks. (28:30)

Franklin's favorite truths.

When the well is dry, we know the worth of the water.

There was never a good knife made of bad steel.

Diligence is the mother of good luck. (37:30)

Lost time is never found again. (44:30)

## #116 — #116 Sam Bronfman (Seagram's and the Bronfman family dynasty)

Founders #116: Sam Bronfman (Seagram's)

Born in 1891, Bronfman was the human inflection point.

One person. One life. He took his family from rags on the Canadian frontier to a liquor empire that shaped generations.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

One person can change the trajectory of an entire bloodline.
Sam was born in Russia, raised in poverty, and built something that still echoes today. Intense drive + strategic vision = a permanent pivot for your descendants. (3:15)

Outwork them so hard it hurts.
He wanted a hotel with a key liquor license. The owner was six days away by dogsled in the wilderness. Sam didn’t wait. He went himself, freezing, starving, miserable, and closed the deal. (22:30)

Never take the easy money.
Hudson’s Bay Company screwed up their inventory and begged Sam for liquor. He had it. He could’ve price-gouged them into oblivion. Instead he said, “Just pay me back when you can.” Built a relationship with a giant that paid off for decades. (24:30)

Money is a barbell.
Frugal to the point of absurdity in his personal life (lights off, complaints about pencils). But when it came to product quality, marketing, or equipment? He spent like a madman. Cut everything non-essential. Over-invest in what actually moves the needle. (44:45)

Study the survivors, not the spreadsheets.
Sam kept massive inventory buffers. Everyone called it inefficient. Then WWII hit, supply chains collapsed, and his competitors got destroyed. Sam owned the market. Redundancy looks dumb until the black swan shows up. (50:15)

No foot on first base.
When Seagram’s attacked a new market or launched a campaign, it was all-in. No backup plan. No retreat. Sam’s message to the company: “Attack vigorously and never give up until you win.” Hedging is for people who want to stay small. (56:00)

The best whiskey has not been made.
Even when they were #1 in the world, Sam walked the floors telling his distillers: “The best whiskey has not yet been made.” Never rest. Never coast. Obsess over quality until the day you die. (56:15)

@Chancellorpen Exactly.

Bronfman’s life proves relentless effort can break generational poverty for your kids.

Even if the scars of hardship stay with you forever.

## #117 — #117 : Chung Ju-yung founder of Hyundai (the most inspiring autobiography I've read)

Founders #117: Chung Ju Yung (Hyundai)

Chung Ju Yung ate tree bark to survive as a kid.

He died the richest man in Korea.

This is what relentless effort looks like when it never stops.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

Effort is what the people closest to you actually remember.
Accomplishments are nice. But your family? They see the grind. The exhaustion. The daily choice to keep going. Chung’s father farmed from the day he could walk until the day he died, head to toe in the dirt—so his kids could have more. That’s the real legacy. (4:15)

The father who sacrificed everything.
Chung Ju Yung’s dad was a poor farmer who gave his entire life for his children. Birth to death, sunrise to sunset, every single day. The best parents don’t just provide—they pour out their whole existence so the next generation can start higher. (10:00)

Poverty so brutal you eat tree bark.
No rice. No options. They stripped bark off trees just to have something in their stomachs. That was the baseline. Most people would’ve stayed there forever. Chung used it as rocket fuel. (17:00)

Realistic and “cutting corners” do not exist in my world.

Half measures? Compromises? Being practical? None of that was allowed. His motto: Do it until there is nothing left to do. Give it your all until the very end. This was the operating system for his entire life. (23:00)

Show up every day until the system breaks.  
He needed a permit. The bureaucracy said no. So he went to the police station every single day for a month. Same question. Same answer. He didn’t stop until it was done. Do your best, then keep doing more. (29:00)

What bed bugs taught him about winning.
He watched bed bugs get crushed, then come right back. More aggressive than before. “If these bedbugs can persist like this, why can’t we?” Never quit. Never accept defeat. Emulate the bugs. (31:00)

Build at a speed that looks impossible.
Broke ground on the factory. Six months later the first Hyundai car drove off the line. Most companies take years. He did it in months. Speed is a moat. (47:00)

Find something you love and do it until you die.
That was his advice at 80+. Pick the work that lights you up. Then pour every remaining day into it. No retirement. No coasting. Just full commitment until the end. (1:07:00)

Doubts will only let you go as far as they allow.
If you doubt yourself, your results will be capped by those doubts. Believe you can do it first. Then outwork everyone to make the belief true. (1:08:00)

One day of diligence changes the trajectory.
Be diligent for a single day and your life improves. Do it for a month and the difference is obvious. Compound that over years and you’re unstoppable. (1:12:00)

Such a man lives well.
The man who gives his absolute best every day, stays present, dreams of a better future, and finds joy in the smallest tasks, that man doesn’t just succeed. He lives a full, meaningful life. (1:18:00)

This episode will wreck you in the best way if you’re building anything serious.

What’s your favorite lesson here? Drop it below. I’m on to the next one tomorrow.

@Chancellorpen The top ten is so fair. I agree. I need to read his book, it's on my list. Hearing that moved it for sure

## #118 — #118 Forty Years With Henry Ford

Founders #118: Henry Ford - $F

Henry Ford had one obsession.

A car so cheap that the average man could own one.

He didn’t just build the car. 

He built the entire system to make it possible.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

Be ruthless on the goal. Flexible on everything else.
Ford never budged on “a car for the masses.” But the how? Whatever worked. Trial. Error. Scrap it. Start over. Stubborn on the destination, wide open on the route. (31:30)

The answer you need is probably already in the graveyard of old ideas.
Ford took a transmission design everyone had thrown away years earlier, dusted it off, and used it to slash costs. History is full of these moments. The future belongs to people who study the past. (1:09:00)

Your best work comes when the odds are stacked against you.
Ford was at his absolute peak when everyone told him the Model T was impossible. Once he won and the empire was running, his attention wandered. Humans are wired for the fight, not the victory lap. (29:00)

The mission never ends.
After the Model T, Ford needed a new mountain immediately or he started to drift. Same as JFK after the moon landing. Finish one impossible thing, then lock in the next one before momentum dies. (31:00)

Experts are often the enemy of progress.
Ford fired anyone who started calling themselves an expert. Their brains filled up with “that’s impossible.” He wanted people who didn’t know what couldn’t be done. Beginner’s mind wins. (44:00)

New ideas need protection.
Ford literally walled off a secret room on the third floor, put a lock on it, and told Sorensen (the author) “we’re starting something completely new in here.” The Model T was born in a maternity ward. (1:08:00)

## #119 — #119 The Dodge Brothers

Founders #119 Dodge Brothers

Two small-town machinists who supplied the guts of Henry Ford’s empire.

They made the parts everyone else needed during the auto gold rush.

While others chased the dream of building cars, the Dodge brothers made the transmissions, axles, and parts that every early automaker desperately needed.

“In a gold rush, don’t dig for gold. Sell pickaxes.” (24:30)

Today was a shorter episode, more story than insights. Great story.

## #120 — #120 Billy Durant (Creator of General Motors)

Founders #120: Billy Durant (General Motors)

Billy Durant built one of the largest companies in history.

Then lost it. Then built it again. Then lost everything.

His wins were massive. His blind spots were fatal.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

All great things start small.
Empires rarely begin fully funded. A tiny, focused bet that proves the model is enough to compound into something enormous. (15:00)

Let the customer sell themselves.
Durant’s sales rule: Assume they know more than you, talk less, give them time to think. Silence lets the product close the deal. Works only when the product is undeniably better. (19:30)

Your best year can come right before collapse.
1906 was the carriage company’s most profitable ever. Within a decade the entire industry was dead. Record earnings don’t protect against disruption. (33:00)

A big mistake.
Durant rolled up 20+ companies into $GM in two years with no central staff, no uniform accounting, no inventory discipline. It looked brilliant until liquidity dried up. (50:00)

Serial founders just keep starting.
Kicked out of GM? He immediately starts Chevrolet to compete with it. Then Frigidaire. Same vision, new canvas. Market gaps don’t stop calling. (56:30)

95% execution isn’t enough.
He had the businesses, the vision, the scale. One missing piece, financial discipline, cost him control twice and wiped him out in the end. (57:00)

Good times hide overextension.
Durant was the opposite of Hetty Green and Mark Spitznagel. He loaded up in bull markets and had no defense when the cycle turned. “The unusual happens usually” (1:05:00)

This episode is a masterclass in how close world-class success and total ruin can sit.

## #121 — #121 Billy Durant and Alfred Sloan (General Motors)

Founders #121: Alfred Sloan and Billy Durant (General Motors)

Billy Durant built $GM with speed and vision.

Alfred Sloan turned the chaos into the most successful corporation of its time.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

Control the vertical that matters most.

Durant got burned early when a supplier tried to cut him out after he had already sold the product.

That lesson never left him.

Control the critical links in the supply chain or stay vulnerable forever. (21:00)

Raise your standards when you see higher ones.

Henry Leland tore into Sloan’s bearings for poor quality.

Sloan did not defend himself.

He raised his own standards on the spot.

For Leland quality was God. Sloan made it his too. (38:00)

Respect others' time.

Billy Durant treated every minute of his own time as sacred.

Wasting one was an affront to the Divine Creator.

Yet he regularly wasted the time of his best executives.

That hypocrisy drove talent away. (41:00)

Companies die from indigestion more than starvation.

Durant and $GM swallowed more than twenty companies in two years with almost no integration or controls.

The company fought off backruptcy soon thereafter.

David Packard & $HPE were right: more companies die from indigestion than starvation. (49:30)

Go to the front lines yourself.

Sloan traveled the country, sat across dealers’ desks, listened to every complaint and suggestion, took careful notes, and studied them when he got home.

Truth lives at the point of sale. (1:17:00)

Change never stops.

No company ever stopped changing.

Your job is to build a framework that turns constant change into progress instead of destruction. (1:18:00)

@HBO_data 121/411+ done

## #122 — #122 Alfred Sloan (General Motors)

Founders #122: Alfred Sloan (General Motors)

Henry Ford built the car for the masses.

Alfred Sloan built the company that beat him and dominated the 20th century.

This is how he did it.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

Comfort is a false god.

Sloan was already successful with his own thriving business.

Instead of staying comfortable he sold it and stepped into the total chaos of $GM.

He widened his horizon on purpose.

Nothing great happens inside the safe zone. (22:30)

Focus only on what moves the business forward.

Sloan protected his time with ruthless discipline.

Almost everything he did served the core mission.

High performers do not let side issues steal energy from their main craft. (37:00)

Groups make policy. Individuals enforce it.

Committees are useful for debate and direction.

Execution belongs to one accountable person.

Sloan never confused the two. (39:00)

Find your niche and widen it.

Sloan refused to fight Ford head-on at the cheapest price.

He placed Chevrolet just above the Model T and steadily expanded that gap as customers wanted more.

Smart positioning plus patience cracks even the strongest competitor. (47:00)

@Chancellorpen MIT Sloan was named after him for a reason

## #123 — #123 Albert Champion (Record-Setting Racer to Dashing Tycoon)

>be albert champion
>born 1880s paris slums
>pro cyclist to feed family
>crash 20ft in air
>snap femur
>bone out
>almost die
>win title on mangled leg
>pour winnings into us cars
>meet car boss
>move to michigan in 48hrs
>build an empire

stop planning. just act.

founders #123:

In a world overloaded with frameworks and opinions the cure is simple: Take a small number of ideas that actually matter and execute them every single day.

"Champion's nature was to plunge with formidable energy headlong into life and work without pausing for introspection." (22:30)

Lock the past away to protect your focus.

Champion had been a world champion cyclist. 

The day he committed to building his spark plug business he took every trophy, clipping, and scrapbook of old victories, put them in a trunk, and locked it. (41:00)

Yesterday’s wins became irrelevant. 

All attention went to the work directly in front of him.

Decide fast and move your entire life.

He met Billy Durant, saw the far bigger opportunity, and two days later agreed to uproot everything and relocate to Flint, Michigan. (49:00)

"A good plan violently executed now is better than a perfect plan executed next week." - General Patton

## #124 — #124 Larry Ellison and Oracle

Founders #124: Larry Ellison - $ORCL

"Life is the only miracle. I don't waste a lot of time. I work intensely on things that I care about, and I spend my time with people that I care about. I do what I want because there's not much time left."

Here is how to be Livin' like Larry

David Senra described Larry Ellison as the MJ of B2B SaaS, working competitively and living his life just as hard when out of it.

He needed enemies to stay sharp.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

Choose Independence.
"I love golden retrievers, but I don't want to be one. I'd much rather be a cat." Dogs obey for food. Cats survive on their own. He rejected blind loyalty and chose independence. (7:30)

Pure misfit.
When told he would look back on his school days as the best years of his life he replied:
"If that's true, I'm going to kill myself right now." (10:30)

Solve problems fast. 
Contract programming let him finish work quicker than anyone, wear what he wanted, ride his motorcycle, and spend time on what he actually enjoyed. (13:00)

The executives are not geniuses. 
He expected brilliant leaders at the top of tech. Instead he found fear, bureaucracy, and paralysis. That gave him the confidence to run his own company. (17:00)

The harder the task the less competition.
He deliberately chose to build the first commercial relational database because everyone else thought it was too risky and impossible. He picked the path others called impossible. (23:30)

All that matters is what is right, not who.
On Bill Gates: "All he cared was what was right, not who was right. That's what makes Bill very, very dangerous." (1:02:00)

Protect the core team.
Turnover is healthy in many areas, but never on the small group building the main product. That accumulated knowledge compounds for decades and cannot be replaced. (56:00)

You cannot lead with uncertainty.
"You cannot lead if you're filled with uncertainty." People follow conviction, not hesitation. (1:08:00)

## #125 — #125 Charles Kettering (inventor, engineer, founder)

Founders #125: Charles Kettering - $GM

He was a poor farm boy who became one of the greatest inventors of the 20th century and the longtime head of research at General Motors.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

An Ounce of Experimentation Is Worth a Pound of Theory
“The destruction of a theory is of no consequence for theories are only stepping stones. In my opinion, an ounce of experimentation is worth a pound of theory.” 
Build, test, fail, adjust. Kettering hated committees because they slowed down real work. (49:00)

Knowing vs Understanding
There is a massive difference between memorizing facts and truly understanding how things work. Kettering learned real engineering from the practical men running the steam engine at the local flour mill, not from any classroom. (14:00)

Improve It Every Single Time
The actor who played Rip Van Winkle for decades taught him the secret to staying passionate: “I made up my mind that I would try to portray Rip Van Winkle just a little bit better each time.” 
That constant effort at improvement kept his interest alive for life. (20:00)

Gain Altitude to Kill Ego
“Everyone ought to take a ride in an airplane. If the general manager of some great factory reaches an altitude of 5,000 feet and sees his factory the size of a postage stamp, he is bound to realize that he is not so much after all.” 
Perspective is the best cure for self-importance. (46:00)

@Chancellorpen Great pull. It was an awesome moment.

## #126 — #126: Larry Ellison (The Billionaire and the Mechanic)

Founders #126: Larry Ellison - $ORCL

“Your garden is not complete until there's nothing else you can take out of it.” - Ellison

Larry applied it to life: remove everything unnecessary so only the people and work that truly matter remain.

Here’s the three best ideas from the episode:

Use Competition to Test Yourself
Larry treated competition as a deliberate tool to measure and expand his limits, whether in business battles or extreme sports like the America’s Cup (top sailing tournament). (16:00)

Seek Out Alternative Stress
Even as one of the richest men alive he refused a relaxed life. His dangerous hobbies were a “constant search for alternative stress” to keep the same edge he had when fighting for survival. (20:00)

Born With the Right Disadvantages
“I was born with all the disadvantages needed to succeed.” His difficult early life gave him exactly the hunger and resilience required to build an empire. (1:01:00)

@Chancellorpen Replace sailing with golf and it’s a carbon copy

## #127 — #127 Larry Ellison (Oracle)

Founders #127: Larry Ellison - $ORCL

Larry Ellison had a very direct way of dealing with problems: Find a way or make one.

Here’s what I pulled from the episode:

Break on through (to the other side).
A programmer said it was impossible to run cables through the wall. Ellison grabbed a hammer and smashed straight through it. That single act summed up his entire business philosophy. 
Find a way or make one. Just do it. (30:00)

Good marketing beats better tech.
“Aver­age technology and good marketing beat good technology and average marketing every day.” He put sales, distribution, and market share first instead of waiting for the perfect product. (36:00)

Engage in an enlightened pursuit of happiness.
Advice to his son, but really to all of us: “He has to engage in an enlightened pursuit of happiness to figure out what makes him happy. That’s all about how you feel about yourself, how you relate to other people, what your work is, what you create and what you make. We are builders. Human beings are builders. He’s going to have to find something he really wants to build.” (58:00)

This was a strong close to the Livin' like Larry series.

@CiaoDottoressa why do you say that?

## #128 — #128 Henry Leland (Cadillac)

Founders #128: Henry Leland (Cadillac / Lincoln)

Henry Leland’s standards were so high that other car tycoons chose to drive his Cadillacs instead of the cars from their own companies.

Here are his three best ideas:

Sweat Blood for Superior Work
“It is easy to get cooperation for mediocre work, but one must sweat blood for the chance to produce a superior product.” 

Leland rejected half his own output when starting his foundry because it was not good enough. (30:00)

Sell By Teaching
Leland never acted like a salesman. 

He visited factories, taught managers how to use machines better, and often improved their entire manufacturing system. 

Orders followed naturally. (20:00)

Master Yourself First
“The one essential ingredient of success was mastery of one’s self as well as one’s job.” 

Technical skill without strong character and self-discipline was worthless to him. (45:00)

Bonus Lesson on Investing an Nascent Industries.
About 2,000 automobile companies emerged in the U.S. between 1894 and 1930, but fierce competition, the Great Depression, and economies of scale narrowed the industry down to just three major players:

1) General Motors, $GM; 2) Ford, $F; and 3) Chrysler, $STLA.

Leland’s focus on precision is why Cadillac-- which helped GM survive tough times-- was able to make it. (33:00)

@Chancellorpen The end is rather tragic. Ford kinda screws over Leland, who fought hard into his 80s to get retribution for his shareholders. Always two sides to the story, though.

@Chancellorpen @FoundersPodcast Spot on. Ford has some skeletons to say the least… Also has some of the best ideas about business.

## #129 — #129 Felix Dennis (How to Get Rich)

Founders #129: Felix Dennis (How to Get Rich)

If you do not believe in yourself at a fundamental level, no one else ever will.

It’s a prerequisite to success.

If money doesn’t drive you, learn his ideas. And if it does, well…

Here are Felix’s best ideas:

Full commitment unlocks clairvoyance.
"The moment that one commits oneself, providence moves too... Whatever you do or dream you can, begin it." - Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (1749-1832)

Hesitation kills more ideas than anything else. (25:00)

“Rich by accident” is a myth.
Wealth never arrives by lucky accident or by simply following your passion and hoping for the best. 

It is the result of a deliberate, sustained decision to go after it with everything you have. (13:00)

Act without fear of loss.
"You have almost nothing. And therefore, you have almost nothing to lose."

All the great founders leverage this. (16:00)

Never trust the vast mountain of conventional wisdom.
"Conventional wisdom daunts initiative and offers far too many convenient reasons for inaction, especially for those with a great deal to lose."

The status quo exists mostly to protect people who already have something to lose. 

The rare times it is wrong are exactly where the biggest fortunes are made by those willing to ignore it. (17:00)

Life is not a rehearsal.
Wasting decades in work you hate just because other people told you it was the safe or respectable choice is one of the saddest things a person can do. (33:00)

Only you know your inner demons.
"Do not mistake desire for compulsion. Only you can know the song of your inner demons."

The grind required to get truly rich is brutal and long. That level of drive cannot be copied from someone else. (36:00)

The chase for wealth is absurd.
"If you chase money desperately in the earnest belief that you can never be happy without it and seriously think that the chase is a meaningful occupation, I doubt very much you will succeed… an appreciation of the absurdity of the chase helps enormously." (46:00)

Sell Up and Change Your Life
Time is the only asset you can never replace. 

Staying in a business or career that makes you miserable to keep making money is a form of self-inflicted insanity. 

Get out the moment you realize it. (52:00)

Ownership is half the law.
"If on my deathbed, I had only a very short time to pass, to pass on what wisdom I have accumulated about getting rich to a son or daughter, then it would be this. Ownership shall be half the law, doing an outstanding job should be the other half. There's no point in owning 100% of a rubbish company." (54:00)

If you made it this far, thank you for taking the time to read this. It took a while to compile, so I hope you found it as helpful as I have.

@Chancellorpen Yet to read the book but I came a similar conclusion over time.

## #130 — #130 Walter Chrysler

Founders #130: Walter Chrysler

In 1901, Chrysler saved just $60 at age 26 while earning 30 cents an hour. Fifteen years later, he was earning $600,000 a year as the highest-paid person in the automobile industry. (23:00)

Things can improve in your life much faster than you think.

Here is how Chrysler went zero to one.

Prioritize learning over comfort: He constantly left jobs he had already mastered to find harder challenges. He spent years traveling from town to town as a mechanic just to study different engines, and later took a 50% pay cut to jump from the established locomotive business into the raw, unproven automobile industry.

Obsessive self-education: To understand the new technology of automobiles, he went into massive debt to buy a $5,000 luxury car. Instead of driving it, he spent months completely dismantling and rebuilding it until he understood every single mechanical function.

Cross-pollinate industries: Chrysler's ultimate value came from applying the strict cost tracking and precise assembly schedules he mastered in the mature railroad industry to the chaotic early auto industry, cutting car production times from four days to just fifteen minutes. (55:00)

Other great ideas:

Never lose your temper on paper.
As a quick tempered young superintendent Chrysler wrote an angry letter after a rebuke from the general master mechanic. 

His boss Mr. Hickey called him in and taught him the rule: file angry letters in a drawer for a few days to cool off before you read them again.

An emotional reply on paper (email, text, etc.) becomes a permanent record of a temporary feeling that can wreck your career. 

A mandatory cooling off period keeps you logical and protects every important relationship. (29:00)

Origin story of the most beautiful building in the NYC skyline.
Chrysler wanted his sons to have real responsibility once they settled in New York. 

Remembering a structure from Paris he told the architect to build something taller than the Eiffel Tower. 

Then he made his son start by scrubbing floors in the basement so he would understand the entire operation before he ever managed it. (1:05:00)

Maintain gratitude for your humble beginnings.
In the postscript his co author reveals that Chrysler frequently cried when he remembered his early days as a poor roving mechanic.

He wanted his story to inspire other lonely ambitious boys still trying to find their way. (1:07:00)

@nickktrannn Count me in

## #132 — #132 Edwin Land (Steve Jobs's Hero)

Founders #132: Edwin Land - Polaroid

Edwin Land was no ordinary founder.

He was the mentor to Steve Jobs.

And helped design the U-2 spy plane.

Here are Land’s best ideas:

If anything is worth doing, it is worth doing to excess.

Land approached his goals with intense passion, living this quote throughout his life. "There's a rule that they don't teach you at the Harvard Business School. It is, if anything is worth doing, it's worth doing to excess." (8:00)

Believe a market will come to exist for any worthwhile product.

Land prioritized creating the best products over immediate market demand. (15:00)

Do not do anything that someone else can do.

This motto guided Land's life and Polaroid's focus on originality. They even scrapped projects if competitors got there first. (21:00)

Be scientifically daring and financially conservative.

Land ran Polaroid with massive scientific risks but zero outside debt. He was able to maintain full creative control. (46:00)

Strive to know more than anyone else about your particular craft.

Like the 17-year-old Land who read every optics book in the library.

Study the history and know the pioneers. (50:00)

Be comfortable saying I do not know.

Despite his intense control, Land showed intellectual humility. He admitted unknowns without hesitation. (52:00)

The value of concentration.
"I find it very important to work intensively for long hours when I am beginning to see solutions to a problem. At such times, atavistic competencies seem to come welling up..." (54:00)

Everything is connected.

Land viewed business, happiness, and doing good as one holistic pursuit.

Not separate compartments.

He does not see business as this separate thing.

He sees everything is related to everything else. (57:00)

@Chancellorpen The next two episodes are about him as well.

Looking forward to writing the posts. This one was a lot of fun.

## #133 — #133 Edwin Land (Polaroid and The Man Who Invented It)

Founders #133: Edwin Land - Polaroid

Land dedicated 30 years to perfecting his revolutionary camera.

Like his mentee, Steve Jobs, Land's life shows that monumental success requires decades of relentless persistence and a refusal to accept the status quo.

Here are more of Land’s best ideas:

Remember that you will die someday, and your fear will die today.
"Almost everything, all external expectations, all pride, all fear of embarrassment or failure, these things just fall away in the face of death, leaving only what is truly important." - Land (6:00)

Nature is indifferent to your struggles. 
The physical world yields only to ingenuity and imagination, not effort or wishful thinking

"Nature offered no bar to the elegant solution over the awkward compromise if they had the imagination to seek it and the wit to discover it." - Land (8:00)

Bet on the great individual, not the group. 
Progress leaps forward through obsessed outliers who compress lifetimes of advancement into one career.

This podcast is evidence of such phenomenon. (17:00)

Question everything like a child. 
Unquestioned norms hide massive opportunities. Test cliches and reject conventional wisdom. (31:00)

Edwin Land on taste.
"Taste is as rare as the unicorn." - Land (41:00)

Make learning a lifelong duty.
"The individual and industry will be better qualified... if he continues his education as an integral part of his working career." - Land (43:00)

## #134 — #134 Edwin Land (Polaroid vs Kodak)

Founders #134: Edwin Land - Polaroid

Edwin Land viewed his company as an extension of his scientific soul.

When Polaroid's survival was at risk, Land saw suing Kodak a sacred duty to defend their innovations.

Here are ideas from Land's legendary patent lawsuit:

Defend your foundation without apology.
Kodak tried to crush Polaroid by stealing their instant film patents.

Land fought back by taking the stand and delivering a captivating lesson in optics that left the judge captivated.

Adjusted for inflation, the case would be one of the largest payouts in patent law history ($2.2B).

"Our manifest duty to our shareholders is vigorously to assert our patents." - Land (11:00)

Never underestimate worthy rivals.
Land respected Kodak's prowess despite his advantages, while they fatally dismissed the obsessive creator. (55:00)

Tackle impossible challenges. 
Land built the Polaroid camera around ambitious idea of fulfilling the unarticulated human need to see a photograph instantly. The idea came from his daughter asking to see a picture that was just taken.  (3:00)

Guard against stultification.
Avoid those whose spark has dulled. "They have been stultified somehow and the world is going by them." (15:00)

Begin with the ideal vision, work backwards. 
"You always start with a fantasy. Part of the fantasy technique is to visualize something as perfect. Then with the experiments you work back from that fantasy to reality, hacking away at the components." - Land (21:30)

Frame business as bold experimentation. 
Innovation thrives on embracing failure as a path to success, enduring setbacks with resilience. An essential aspect of creativity is not being afraid to fail. (22:30)

Dismiss mockery from incumbents. 
Revolutionary tools often start as "toys" in the eyes of experts, but they redefine industries. (25:00)

Leverage your advantages beyond obvious markets.
Land's optics expertise birthed the U-2 spy plane, proving founders can reshape history in unexpected arenas. (28:00)

## #135 — #135 Joseph Pulitzer (Politics & Media)

Founders #135: Joseph Pulitzer

"I think God Almighty made it for the benefit of the world when He made me blind."

Pulitzer rose from extreme poverty to media titan, using the press as a weapon for reform in a life marked by triumph that his sudden blindness failed to slow down.

Pulitzer's life was ludacris. Contemporary criticism of media traces back to the seeds Pulitzer planted:

Epic clash with Teddy Roosevelt.
Pulitzer and Roosevelt fiercely battled over America's reform movement, each convinced they alone could save the republic, showcasing the immense power media barons wielded a century ago. (5:00)

Childhood scarred by relentless loss. 
Losing his father and multiple siblings in quick succession as a boy instilled a lifelong fear of death and hypochondria, fueling his drive to succeed while haunting him with deep-seated anxiety. (10:00)

Penniless after the Civil War, Pulitzer was kicked out of a New York hotel lobby where he tried to sleep, only to buy the same building later in life.(14:00)

News (and guns) as a political weapon. 
Pulitzer rejected neutral reporting, viewing newspapers as partisan tools to champion viewpoints and drive change, laying the groundwork for opinion-driven journalism as a lever of influence. (24:00)

As a politician and publisher, Pulitzer fired shots at a corrupt contractor who insulted him. (26:00)

Hypocrisy in the fight against corruption. 
Despite railing against graft to build his reputation, Pulitzer accepted a lucrative government sinecure from an ally, reminding us that even visionary founders fall prey to the flaws they decry in others. (28:00)

Media manipulation as a timeless danger. 
Powerful publishers once colluded to spread lies and derail a political candidate, proving that coordinated misinformation is an age-old tactic, urging skepticism toward media biases today. (32:00)

Crowdfunding the Statue of Liberty. 
When funding stalled for the statue's base, Pulitzer rallied readers with a penny-drive, printing every donor's name to blend civic pride with status appeal, saving the project and boosting his circulation. (54:00)

Relentless self-promotion fuels dominance.
Even at peak success, Pulitzer never eased on aggressive marketing, sharing the mindset of top founders that constant selling is essential to sustain an empire beyond just a great product. (57:00)

Brevity wins reader attention, still does too.
To outpace rivals, Pulitzer mandated short sentences, paragraphs, and stories, grasping early that concise writing expands reach in an era of limited attention spans. (1:02:00)

A+ episode.

@Chancellorpen That's a great line. Very fitting for Pulitzer.

## #136 — #136 Estée Lauder

Founders #136: Estée Lauder

Estée Lauder built a beauty empire through bold risks, innovative simplicity, and unyielding persistence.

Lauder implanted habits into the beauty industry that still exist today.

Here are Estée’s best ideas:

"Risk-taking is the cornerstone of empires. No one ever became a success without taking chances." (16:00)

On the ideas that built her beauty empire.
"It was simple. Most good ideas sparkle in simplicity, so much that everyone wonders why no one ever did that before." (29:00)

Some of the simple ideas she pioneered:

Word-of-mouth sales.
She relied on supreme confidence in the quality of her products rather than flashy marketing campaigns.

Free samples.
By simplifying the sales process to simply letting women try the cream, the product effectively sold itself.

Consumer habits.
Women did not typically buy their own beauty products due to social norms, but Lauder changed that through marketing. This simple shift in perception turned a rare luxury gift into a daily household staple, driving massive sales volume. (33:00)

On perseverance.
"It's that quality that forces you to persevere. Find the route around the stone wall. It's the immovable stubbornness that will not allow you to cave in when everyone says, give up." - Lauder (31:00)

How she stopped corporate espionage.
"I never give the whole formula. When the mixture is 95% or even 98% completed and the great vats of perfume are ready to be bottled, one member of the Lauder family goes to the factory to supply the missing secret 5% or 2%, the ingredient without which the fragrance can never be complete." (35:00)

@SpiceP0dcast Great shout! @SpiceShorts

@SpiceP0dcast @SpiceShorts @FoundersPodcast When I get to video episodes I’ll definitely check it out!

## #137 — #137 P.T. Barnum

Founders #137: P.T. Barnum

“The noblest art is that of making others happy.”

Barnum was the greatest showman of the 19th-century.

He built his fortune by spotting contradictions in human behavior and turning them into dollars.

Here are Barnum’s best ideas:

Watch actions, not words.
Barnum is running a highly profitable lottery business in a strict, religious New England town. 

He notices that the same devout citizens and church leaders who publicly condemn gambling are secretly his best customers.

Always consider the speaker's incentives. (10:00)

P.T. on effort.
"A person must make 30 hours out of 24 or he will never get ahead." - P.T. Barnum (36:00)

Stay in your circle of competence.
Barnum went bankrupt by investing outside his entertainment expertise.

He later worked his way back up, and became a charitable man. (48:00)

## #138 — #138 Alexander Graham Bell

Founders #138: Alexander Graham Bell

He refused to have a telephone installed in his own study.

The man who invented it understood better than anyone that the device itself was the greatest threat to the concentration required to invent it.

Here are some more ideas from Bell:

Trust your own judgment above all.
"A man's own judgment should be the final appeal in all that relates to himself." (10:00)

Connect ideas from unrelated fields.
Bell's powerful imagination led him to synthesize concepts across domains, culminating in the telephone.

You do not need to be classically trained in a single field if you have the imagination to combine separate concepts into a novel solution. (14:00)

Choose a spouse who complements your weaknesses.
Bell's steady wife grounded his passionate ups and downs, handling what he lacked just as crucial business partners did. (40:00)

Bell was a tremendous father.
"He believed that play is nature's method of educating a child and that a parent's duty is to aid nature in the development of her plan." Foster curiosity and exploration over rigid authority. (50:00)

@Chancellorpen I cannot believe he isn’t talked about more. He won at life. 

Immigrant, world-defining inventor, great family man, etc.

## #139 — #139 J.P. Morgan

Founders #139: J.P. Morgan

Morgan's cynicism helped him navigate finance by always seeking hidden motives behind actions.

Morgan understood this because he was his own example.

Before the Federal Reserve existed, the United States relied on Morgan to act as its central bank:

He gained all his power throughout his life by understanding one truth:

Power comes from others' weakness.
He saved while others spent, knowing human nature would eventually cause economic crashes, which he exploited with his preserved war chest of capital.

Morgan reorganized nearly every bankrupt railroad east of the Mississippi during the 1893 recession, amassing revenues rivaling half the U.S. government's. (41:00)

In the Panic of 1907, the semi-retired Morgan bailed out trusts, brokerages, New York City, and the stock exchange, proving that authority stems from being the lender of last resort. (53:00)

At his peak, Morgan controlled 72 directorships across 112 corporations, prompting a congressional investigation into the sheer scale of his monopoly.

People have good, real reasons.
"A man always has two reasons for the things he does: a good one and the real one." (52:00)

@Chancellorpen I found the episode more storytelling than insight, and his story was not as compelling as his peers' at the time.

## #140 — #140 Bill Gates (the Making of the Microsoft Empire)

Founders #140: Bill Gates

Gates viewed every missed contract as a double loss: revenue lost to Microsoft and opportunities given to competitors. (43:00)

My take: Treat opportunities as if you're trying to make the playoffs, playing every game against your division rival.

Work with people who are hardcore.
“It was when we got that free time that we really got into computers... Then I became hardcore. It was day and night.” 

Gates was 13 at the time. (10:00)

Surround yourself with people who will push you.
While at Harvard, Gates was hesitant about fully committing to starting a software company. Despite his obvious passion for computers, he was paralyzed.

Friend and future co-founder Paul Allen showed Gates a magazine featuring the Altair 880, which marked the beginning of the personal computer revolution. (18:00)

Allen cited a quote from Shakespeare's Julius Caesar that convinced him to start Microsoft:

“There is a tide in the affairs of men, which, taken at the flood, leads on to Fortune. Omitted, all the voyage of their life is bound in shallows and in miseries.” - William Shakespeare

Gates on complacency.
“Believe me... Staring out the window and saying, ‘isn't this great,’ is not the way to push things forward. You've got to keep driving hard.” (54:00)

## #141 — #141 Arnold Schwarzenegger (My Unbelievably True Life Story)

Founders #141: Arnold Schwarzenegger

Moving to the US, he had one year in LA to prove himself, developing an righteous mindset.

"My mission in America was clear. I was on a path. I needed to train like hell, diet like hell, eat well and win."

The power of deadlines.

Here are three more you should know:

Reps are everything.
Juggling construction, business, and college in LA, Arnold drilled posing transitions obsessively to avoid any slip in competitions. 

Raw repetition builds skill where talent falls short. When you have talent and raw repetition you get Arnold.

"You're not born good at something... you have to practice, practice, practice. You practice every pose and every transition because that extra step is the very thing that can make you lose in front of the judges." (26:00)

Lucille Ball's advice for Arnold. I love this.
"Just remember, when they say no, you hear yes and act accordingly. Someone says to you, we can't do this movie, and you hug him and say, thank you for believing in me." (34:00)

Forgive your parents.
Arnold's father was abusive, but he learned to forgive and let go through understanding.

"Would I have left Austria? Probably not, and that is my great fear. I became Arnold because of what he did to me. I recognized that I could channel my upbringing in a positive way rather than complain." (59:00)

Bonus:

"Every day do something that scares you." 

- Eleanor Roosevelt

(1:02:00)
