## #142 — #142 Teddy Roosevelt and J.P. Morgan

Founders #142: Teddy Roosevelt vs J.P. Morgan

Today, many people confuse a rising market with a healthy society.

Roosevelt believed instead that the economy must serve the citizens first:

"Government stands for manhood first and for business only as an adjunct of manhood."

Morgan viewed the economy first.
In his final years, amid criticism and economic turmoil, Morgan held onto his father's advice about America's endless growth.

"There may be times when things are dark and cloudy in America... remember that the growth of that vast country will take care of it all." (51:00)

The strong rule the weak, but the wise rule the strong.
Morgan reorganized nearly every bankrupt railroad east of the Mississippi, amassing revenues that rivaled half the U.S. government's.

It seemed every poorly run railroad fell under Morgan's control, except James J. Hill.

Despite being a smaller business, Hill was a wise operator and withstood the financial pressure of the recession.

Hill became a co-architect with Morgan in creating the Northern Securities Trust, securing his generational wealth. (16:00)

How to be wise.
Hill's relentless pursuit of knowledge gave him an edge in a highly competitive market.

For years, he would sleep at every stop on his routes each day, staying close to the customer around the clock.

Extra take: These are the types of people you'll compete with in the hottest industries (AI, fintech, healthcare, etc.), and the ones who will survive when the sky is falling.

- From my notes on episode #96 about Hill.

Two stubborn sides cost everyone more.
The 1902 coal strike lasted 164 days, bleeding millions in wages and profits as unions and owners refused to compromise.

Teddy (supporting the miners) and Morgan couldn't reach an agreement, mostly because of their egos, leading to unnecessary value loss for the common people. (42:00)

## #143 — #143 Alfred Lee Loomis (the most interesting man you've never heard of)

Founders #143: Alfred Lee Loomis

Before the US recognized the Nazi threat, a former banker invested his fortune to establish a research lab, which led to the development of microwave radar.

Loomis's early foresight and investments completely changed the course of WWII.

Capitalize on inevitabilities.
As worries about the economy increased in the 1920s, Loomis treated the Great Depression as an inevitability rather than a timing exercise. Loomis did not try to predict the exact date of the crash to squeeze out a few extra dollars.

He recognized the inevitable. Had greed gotten the best of Loomis, the radar breakthrough would at least have been delayed. (18:00)

The radar won the war, and the atomic bomb ended it.
Loomis used his money and connections to cut through the patent disputes and red tape that usually stall fast-moving projects. By handling the administrative headaches himself, he allowed his top scientists to concentrate solely on building the radar.

Many who were involved in developing the radar went on to work in the Manhattan Project. (13:00)

Influence the influencers.
To build his secret lab team, Loomis first recruited Nobel laureate Ernest Lawrence, using his prestige to attract elite physicists who might otherwise ignore a financier. Once Lawrence joined, many followed. (36:00)

@Chancellorpen When 20th century Ben Franklin and real life Batman are your two comps… insane.

## #144 — #144 Ernest Shackleton

Founders #144: Ernest Shackleton

Leader of history's greatest survival story.

Stuck in Antarctica with 28 men, 1,200 miles from civilization, world's most dangerous seas between them and land.

Shackleton led them all home. Nobody died under his watch.

Here are some insights:

Nominative determinism.
“By endurance we conquer” was his family motto.

Shackleton renamed his ship to Endurance. (17:00)

Purpose turns dread into drive.
“I pray to God I can keep the whole party safe.”
Journaling before the ice trek, Shackleton bared his crushing responsibility for every life.

Outward confidence masks the leader's solitary burden of duty. (26:00)

Mindset makes hell heaven, or vice versa.
Amid stalled progress and retreats, Shackleton battled despair harder than ice or cold, knowing mindset turns ordeals survivable or fatal. Guard hope above all.

"Shackleton was concerned, of all the enemies, the cold, the ice, and the sea, he feared none more than demoralization." - Senra (30:00)

Survive by mastering the next step alone.
Like SEAL training wisdom, Shackleton's crew endured by focusing solely on immediate actions, not the vast journey. Break your epic into steps. (41:00)

@Chancellorpen Story hits every time. Going to read his book soon.

## #145 — #145 William Randolph Hearst

Founders #145: William Randolph Hearst

Hearst realized news could be sold as theater.

He built a media machine that reached millions and shaped what people read, feared, wanted, and argued about.

It helped push the country toward the Spanish-American War.

Three key takeaways you should know:

Invest in top talent.
His father struck a fortune in a gold mine, giving William a safer setup than Pulitzer.

He advocated to his father, George, urging him to change the strategy for the San Francisco Examiner.

William is arguing against his father's frugality in staffing, insisting that hiring top-tier employees is necessary for a successful turnaround.

This talent focus became a staple of his empire, as he could scale his talent's output across media (print, radio, film, etc.) to achieve a unique economy of scope benefit.

Hearst understood that acquiring top human capital, even at a massive premium, was the fastest way to build a competitive moat and transform a failing asset into a market leader. (13:00)

Copy proven ideas from competitors.
Instead of trying to invent a new playbook from scratch, he looks directly to Joseph Pulitzer's successful strategies in New York.

Costco, Walmart, and Amazon all copied each other, deliberately taking the best ideas from one another and from competitors. Also, on a personal level, it reminds us that just because someone is already building something similar doesn’t mean you can’t do it too. (25:00)

He didn't learn from his past, and it cost him everything.
Decades of unchecked spending, massive debt accumulation, and a refusal to cut costs during the Great Depression finally catch up to Hearst in his seventies.

His complete lack of capital discipline forces his creditors to step in and seize control of his entire media empire to avoid total liquidation. (59:00)

Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it,” - George Santayana

## #146 — #146 Milton Hershey (Chocolate)

Founders #146: Milton Hershey

“After all, what good is one's money unless one uses it for the good of the community and humanity in general.”

This philosophy separated him from other industrialists and cemented his utopian legacy through the sustainable community and school he built.

Here are Milton Hershey’s best ideas:

Rock bottom eliminates fear of failure.
After two major business collapses left him broke and in debt, Hershey found liberation in having nothing left to lose, which fueled his bold third attempt without hesitation. (24:00)

Compounding small improvements creates miracles.
Hershey built his caramel business through obsessive daily tweaks to quality and operations, expanding market share year after year without a single breakthrough moment. (31:00)

Perfect essentials for ultimate scale.
By simplifying his chocolate production to focus on just a few core items, Hershey achieved massive efficiency and low costs that outpaced competitors.

Limiting complexity allows total mastery of what matters most. (41:00)

## #147 — #147 Sam Colt

Founders #147: Samuel Colt

From selling laughing gas on the street to inventing the revolver and leading the American arms industry, Colt packed a lifetime of extreme hustle, failures, and wins into just 47 years.

Here is the same advice Sam shared with his brother:

Choose a goal and pursue it to the absolute peak.
“Select the object of your ambition and reach its zenith of your hope or die in the attempt.” - Sam Colt

At 32, after a decade of setbacks, Colt told his brother to select your ambition and reach its zenith or die trying. (48:00)

Study human nature deeply to unlock success.
“Life is a thing to be enjoyed. Make up your mind determinedly what station in life you will reach and rely upon it with proper exertion, and you will not be thwarted. Your great study should be of man.”

Understanding people and psychology became the real leverage for selling inventions and building an empire. (49:00)

Never let anyone else govern your destiny.
I love this one: “Lose no opportunity to mingle with the mass and view nature in its most primitive state. If you allow other people to govern you, you subscribe yourself inferior to them. Don't, for the sake of your own good name, think again of being a subordinate.”

Scarred by early authority and a failed board-run company, Colt warned his brother that allowing others to control you marks you as inferior. Absolute self-determination drove him to make every decision in his second company. (48:00)

Aim high or die trying. Mediocrity is unacceptable.
Colt ended his letter to his brother with brutal clarity: “You had better blow out your brains at once and manure an honest man's ground with your carcass than to hang your ambition on such a low peg.”

Better to end it all than hang your ambition on a low peg. (50:00)

## #149 — #149 The Big Rich (Oil Billionaires)

Founders #149: Roy Cullen, Clint Murchison & H.L. Hunt

These Texas wildcatters turned years of dry holes, massive debt, and expert skepticism into some of the greatest independent fortunes in history by simply refusing to quit and betting big on their own convictions.

Here are their best ideas:

Never quit on your family, no matter how hard it gets.
Cullen pushed through three straight years of brutal exhaustion and repeated failures at Damon Mound. His daily promise to his wife that tomorrow would be another day kept him going for their children’s future. (18:00)

Go deeper where everyone else stops. 
Cullen’s entire edge came from drilling further into wells that competitors had already abandoned. This simple “a little deeper” rule became his lifelong strategy and literally defined him until the grave. (20:00)

Your industry can be bigger than entire nations. 
During WWII, Texas alone produced nearly double the oil output of all Axis powers combined. These independent founders operated on a scale that literally fueled world events. (59:00)

## #150 — #150 Sam Walton (America's Richest Man)

Founders #150: Sam Walton
When naming his new stores, Walton deliberately chose "Walmart" because shorter names reduced costs for signs, maintenance, and lighting.

Cost control should be a core part of every decision, both personally and professionally. (40:00)

Here are some of Sam Walton’s best ideas:

Simple models succeed when they are executed with discipline.
Warren Buffett pointed out that a business's difficulty doesn’t determine its success. Walton demonstrated this by building an empire around a single, clear strategy (buy low, sell cheap) and applying it with unmatched consistency. (6:00)

Count your pennies, and your dollars will take care of themselves.
“We don't make a dime out of the merchandise we sell. We only make our profit out of the paper and string we save.” - Sam Walton (15:00)

Create advantages others are unwilling to pursue.
Frustrated by long drives between stores, Walton learned to fly a plane to scout locations and manage his growing network faster than any competitor. The key wasn’t the plane itself, but finding practical edges that rivals refused to pursue. (27:00)

## #151 — #151 Frederick Smith (FedEx)

Founders #151: Frederick Smith - FedEx

He launched one of the era's most insane business ideas.

Smith had to construct the entire national system and aviation fleet before he could handle even a single paying customer.

Three key takeaways from this episode:

Ignorance can give you the courage to start the impossible.
Smith succeeded in part because he did not fully grasp how difficult it would be to build an overnight national delivery network. This lack of complete knowledge kept him from abandoning the project before it began. (26:00)

Sustained focus reveals hidden problem-solving power.
“My whole life has been spent trying to teach people that intense concentration for hour after hour can bring out in people resources they didn't know they had.” - Smith (37:00)

“Timing is often more important than luck.”
Smith observed that his Federal Express concept would have failed five years earlier due to missing infrastructure, and five years later, the opportunity would have been too obvious for others to miss. The exact window proved more important than random chance. (1:05:00)

@Chancellorpen We got the same favorites I swear

## #152 — #152 Katherine Graham (Washington Post)

Founders #152: Katharine Graham - Washington Post

She risked everything to publish the Pentagon Papers and the Watergate story while facing threats from the Nixon admin.

Thrust into leadership after discovering her husband's suicide, she became the first female Fortune 500 CEO.

Here are Katharine Graham’s best ideas:

Let a major crisis divide your life into two.
Her husband’s suicide became the clear turning point that forced her into leadership after years in the background.

Many defining careers have a distinct before-and-after moment. (14:00)

The right teacher compresses years of learning into months.
With almost no business experience, Graham received patient instruction from Warren Buffett, who taught her finance step by step and helped build her confidence. (throughout)

Orangutan Theory.
Warren Buffett served as her sounding board, using Charlie Munger’s “orangutan theory.”

Simply listening while she talked helped her organize her thoughts and remove mental distortions. (57:00)

## #153 — #153 Bill Bowerman (Nike)

Founders #153: Bill Bowerman

The legendary Oregon track coach and Nike co-founder transformed running by rethinking training, shoes, and life itself.

He also once pinned a seven-foot rattlesnake in his driveway with just a clipboard.

Here are Bill Bowerman’s best ideas:

First principles thinking outperforms industry norms.
When the athletic world saw rest as a weakness, Bowerman studied performance from the ground up and demonstrated that strategic recovery was crucial.
This revolutionary approach quickly spread worldwide among elite athletes. (17:00)

Total dedication always carries a personal cost.
Years of experimenting with toxic shoe materials in unventilated spaces left Bowerman with permanent nerve damage and unable to run in the shoes he created for the world. (1:10:00)

Study great lives daily to improve your own.
Just as running every day builds the body, reading biographies every day builds the mind with proven lessons. 
Bowerman and Phil Knight both saw daily habits like these as the way to make the world better. (1:16:00)

## #175 — #175 Theodore Roosevelt's Darkest Journey

When you are in a rut, do something hard.

After losing the 1912 election, Teddy Roosevelt sank into a depression. His cure was to chart an unmapped river in the Amazon.

"If it is necessary for me to leave my bones in South America, I'm quite ready to do so."

Founders #175

Teddy was severely injured and commanded his son to leave him behind to die so the rest of the expedition could survive. 

Kermit disobeys this direct order, completely flipping the dynamic of their relationship.

"For the first time in his life, Kermit simply refused to honor his father's wishes. Whatever it took, whatever the cost, he would not leave without Roosevelt." (1:09:00)

The Incas built one of the greatest imperial states without a system of writing. You must drop preconceived notions of what tools are "necessary". (1:07:00)

You will never look at vines the same. 

Rather than spending resources to build a massive trunk, they survive the Amazon by climbing the giants that already did. Nature proves that competition is simply a signal to specialize. (48:00)

## #187 — #187 Albert Einstein

Einstein, like an founder, was often wrong: 

"He seeks in all directions, so one must expect the majority of the paths on which he embarks to be blind alleys."

In both science and business, progress is inevitable for those who endure 1,000s of failed tests.

Founders #187

"Personal relationships involve nature's most mysterious forces. Outside judgments are easy to make and hard to verify." - Elbert Einstein

The logical reasoning that uncovers the laws of the universe often fails to resolve the volatile nature of human emotions. (44:00)

Men are prone to resist confinement.
To Einstein, marriage was confining, which was a state he instinctively resisted. (48:00)

“Your kids don’t care how successful you are... They just want your time.” - David Senra (49:00)

## #188 — #188 Joe Coulombe (Founder of Trader Joes)

Trying to find an optimal solution in business is a waste of time. 

The factors in the equation are changing all the time. You've got to have something to hang your hat on. 

The one core value that I chose was our high-wage policies.

- Joe Coulombe, Trader Joe's

Founders #188

Business environments are too dynamic to perfectly optimize.

If you adopt a reasonable strategy rather than waiting for an optimal one and stick with it, you'll probably succeed. Tenacity is as important as brilliance. - Coulombe (22:00)

The greatest danger was not that the enemy learned your plans but that your own troops would not. - General Patton (40:00)
